title: Willow harvesting and preparation
subtitle: When to cut, how to dry, and soaking schedules for basket rods
excerpt: A guide to harvesting willow from the stool, drying it as brown willow, storing it, and reconstituting it with soaking before use. Understanding the material from the first cut to the first weave.
sourceNotes: Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on Somerset willow growing and preparation. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for willow preparation and storage schedules.

glossaryTerms.definition: For willow: freshly cut rods still high in sap moisture. Can be worked immediately without soaking, within three to five days of cutting.
Willow dried in its bark after cutting, retaining a warm brown colour. The standard British basket-making material.

content.text: Willow for basket-making grows on a stool: a permanent rootstock that sends up new rods each year. A stool managed for basketry is cut to the ground every year or every two years. The annual rods are the basket material. Understanding the cutting, drying, and soaking cycle makes all subsequent willow work more reliable.

Willow is cut when the sap is down and the leaves have fallen, from late autumn through to early spring. Cutting in full leaf produces rods that are too high in free moisture and prone to mould during drying. The best window is roughly late November through February.

willow can be worked immediately without soaking. The window is short: within three to five days of cutting, before the rods stiffen. Freshly cut rods are noticeably more pliable than dried-and-soaked rods and smell of sap. If you have access to a coppice, cutting and weaving in the same week produces the most pliable material.

, bundle the freshly cut rods butt-end down in a loose bundle and stand them upright in a sheltered outdoor space. Let them dry naturally for four to six weeks. The rods dry in their bark and take on a warm brown colour. Brown willow is strong enough for storage and working baskets and is the standard material in the British tradition.

Dried rods must be re-soaked before working. The general rule is twelve to twenty-four hours of full submersion for well-dried rods from the current season. Older stock that has been stored for more than one year needs longer: add four hours per year of additional storage. Test readiness by bending a rod through a right angle; if it holds without cracking, it is ready. If it snaps, return it to the trough for another four hours.

Store dried bundles horizontally in a cool, dry space away from direct sunlight. Well-stored bundles keep for several years. Stack bundles off the floor on timber rails to allow air to circulate underneath. Do not store in plastic; condensation inside the wrap promotes mould and ruins the rods.

Before soaking, sort the bundle by butt diameter into three groups: fine (up to 8 mm), medium (8 to 14 mm), and thick (over 14 mm). Fine rods are for weaving; medium for stakes and borders; thick for base sticks and handle bows. Sorting before soaking prevents having to search through a wet trough for the right thickness mid-project.
